file_name: Pangsibidang and Tuba - 2023 - Optimization detection of skin cancer using deep learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented with TensorFlow and Keras, to classify skin cancer images as benign or malignant. The study achieved an accuracy of 78.44% using the 'Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset from Kaggle.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a deep learning classification system for skin cancer. It details specific methodology (using TensorFlow, Keras, and a Convolutional Neural Network), describes the dataset used (Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign from Kaggle), and reports new empirical results (achieving 78.44% accuracy). Although it includes a literature review section, the primary contribution is the implementation and testing of a new diagnostic model, not a summary of prior literature.

title: Optimization Detection of Skin Cancer using Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning classification system to distinguish between benign and malignant skin cancer using images from the 'Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset on Kaggle. The researchers implemented a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using TensorFlow and Keras, utilizing 3,609 training images and 890 test images. The model's performance was evaluated by varying the number of Conv2D layers and training epochs to optimize accuracy. The highest accuracy achieved was 78.44% using a model with 64 nodes in the Conv2D layers trained for 12 epochs. The results indicate that increasing the number of CNN layers and epochs improves classification accuracy for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies images as malignant or benign. The dataset 'Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' contains 1197 malignant images in the training set and 360 malignant (melanoma) images in the test set. The paper explicitly mentions 'malignant melanoma' in the introduction as a form of skin cancer.
The study classifies images as malignant or benign. The dataset contains 1440 benign images in the training set and 300 benign tissue images in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer images into 'benign' or 'malignant' categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the development of a 'classification system for skin cancer' to 'categorize cancer as malignant or benign'. The model uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a Sequential model in Keras to predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant) based on the 'Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset. Performance is measured by accuracy (78.44%) and loss, not continuous regression metrics.

datasets_used.name: Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A public dataset downloaded from Kaggle.com containing digital photographs of skin lesions categorized as benign or malignant.

use.training: Used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model. The paper notes that 2637 shots were taken to collect train data from the available training set.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance, achieving an accuracy of 78.44% with the optimal configuration.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and loss metrics across different model configurations.

Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign.notes: The study evaluated the model by varying the number of Conv2D layers (nodes) and training epochs. The highest accuracy of 78.44% was achieved with 64 nodes in the Conv2D layers trained for 12 epochs. The abstract mentions a rounded accuracy of 78%.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented using Keras Sequential model

architecture_details.layers: Conv2D layers with filter counts of 32 and 64, kernel size (3, 3). Max pooling layers were also used.

training_details.data_augmentation: Image data generator used to convert dataset folder into array

preprocessing_techniques: Image data generator used to convert dataset folder into array
Integration of [0,1] and [1,0]-categorized picture labels and photographs

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to convert the dataset folder into an array for training and testing. The paper mentions using an image data generator in the methodology but does not specify any geometric transforms (e.g., rotation, flipping), color transforms, or specific augmentation parameters (such as zoom range, shear range, or horizontal/vertical flip settings). It primarily served as a data loading mechanism rather than an explicit augmentation technique with defined parameters.

conclusions: The developed deep learning classification system using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented with TensorFlow and Keras achieved an accuracy of 78.44% in categorizing skin cancer images as benign or malignant.
The optimal model configuration consisted of 64 nodes in the Conv2D layers trained for 12 epochs.
Increasing the number of CNN layers (nodes) and training epochs resulted in improved classification accuracy.

Future research should focus on increasing the number of epochs, nodes, and validation steps, as well as expanding the training dataset, to further enhance model accuracy, though this requires specific hardware resources.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, small public dataset (Kaggle's 'Skin cancer: Malignant vs. Benign') with only 4,499 total images, limiting generalizability.
The model achieved a low accuracy of 78.44%, which is insufficient for reliable clinical diagnostic applications.
There is a significant class imbalance in the training set (1,440 benign vs. 1,197 malignant) and test set (300 benign vs. 360 malignant).
The paper lacks external validation, cross-validation strategies, or statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals).

Data leakage risk exists as the methodology describes splitting data into training and test sets but does not explicitly detail how the 890 test images were isolated from the original dataset to prevent overlap.
The study does not report standard dermatological metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, or AUC, making it difficult to assess false negative rates critical for cancer detection.
No data augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping) were explicitly applied beyond using an image data generator for loading, limiting robustness to image variations.
The hardware requirements for training are not specified, though the authors note future work requires 'specific hardware resources'.
